Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd tell myself I'd study on certain days, but then I'd just... not feel like it. Most of my studying happened in bursts, usually when I remembered an assignment was due or when the anxiety got bad enough that I couldn't ignore it anymore. I tried to organize things a few times, but it never stuck for more than a week.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines, but not in a productive way. I'd go days without opening anything course-related, then panic and try to cram everything at once. Sometimes I'd have a whole week where I was dealing with other stuff—work, family things—and studying just fell off completely. Regular times? That wasn't really me this semester.
Q3: Usually it was either guilt or panic that got me to log in. I'd get a notification about something due, or I'd suddenly remember I hadn't checked in for a while and feel that sick feeling in my stomach. Sometimes I logged in just to see how behind I was, which honestly just made me feel worse. It wasn't like I was excited to learn—it was more like damage control.
Q4: Not really, no. There were maybe a couple weeks early on where I tried to make it a habit, checking in every other day. But then life got complicated, I missed a few days, and suddenly it felt like too much effort to catch up. The routine never really formed because I kept breaking it and then feeling too overwhelmed to restart.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me since I'm a visual learner. When I actually watched them, things made more sense than just reading. I also liked when there were diagrams or charts—those stuck in my head better. But honestly, I didn't engage with them as much as I should have because sitting through long videos felt exhausting when I was already stressed.
Q6: I mostly ignored the discussion boards and a lot of the readings. The discussions felt pointless—like I was just posting to post, not actually learning anything. The readings piled up so fast that I stopped trying to keep up with them. I'd tell myself I'd catch up later, but later never came. It just felt like too much.
Q7: Something felt worth my time if it was directly connected to an assignment or exam. If I couldn't see the immediate payoff, I'd skip it. I know that's not the best approach, but when you're already drowning, you have to pick what to save. Quick, visual explanations felt valuable. Long theoretical readings? Not so much.
Q8: Optional activities basically didn't exist for me. If it wasn't required, I wasn't doing it—I barely managed the required stuff. Required activities at least had that external pressure pushing me, but even then I'd procrastinate. Optional things felt like extras I couldn't afford when I was struggling with the basics.
Q9: Yeah, I'd download PDFs and videos to my laptop and then study from there without being connected to the LMS. Sometimes I'd screenshot slides or take notes by hand because staring at screens for too long gave me headaches. I also used YouTube a lot for explanations when the course materials didn't click for me.
Q10: There were times I'd spend hours going over my handwritten notes or rewatching a downloaded lecture, but none of that showed up anywhere. I'd also study with a friend from the course sometimes—we'd meet up and go over concepts together. The LMS doesn't know about any of that. It just sees that I wasn't logged in.
Q11: Definitely. I'd open a video once, download it, and then watch it multiple times offline when I was actually in the mood to focus. Same with readings—I'd download the PDF and highlight things on my tablet. Going back to the LMS every time felt like extra steps I didn't need when I already had the file.
Q12: The hardest times were when my anxiety spiked. There were weeks where I could barely get out of bed, let alone think about coursework. I also had some family stuff going on that drained me emotionally. When you're feeling that low, even opening a laptop feels like climbing a mountain. Everything just piled up.
Q13: All of those things affected me, honestly. The workload felt impossible when I was already behind. Confusion made me avoid topics instead of asking for help. Fatigue was constant because I wasn't sleeping well. And I had a part-time job plus extracurriculars that ate into my time. The LMS became something I dreaded opening.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd log in, click around, maybe start a video, but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. I'd realize I'd been staring at the screen for twenty minutes without absorbing anything. It was like going through the motions without actually being present. My body was there but my brain wasn't.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they mattered. Part of it was anxiety—the bigger something felt, the more I avoided it. Starting felt impossible, so I'd distract myself with other things. I knew I was making it worse, but I couldn't seem to stop the cycle. It's like my brain was protecting me from stress by creating more stress.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd ignore everything until the deadline was right there, then scramble to submit something. It wasn't quality work, but it was something. The LMS probably shows these spikes of activity right before due dates. That's just how I operated—crisis mode.
Q17: Feedback was hit or miss. Sometimes getting a decent grade motivated me briefly, but bad feedback just made me want to disengage more. I'd think, what's the point if I'm already failing? The mindfulness intervention they had us do helped a little with the anxiety, but it didn't really change how I approached the coursework.
Q18: The structure felt pretty rigid, which didn't work well for someone like me who was struggling to keep up. Once I fell behind, there was no easy way to catch up. Weekly modules kept moving forward whether I was ready or not. I needed more flexibility, but the course just kept going at its pace.
Q19: Having everything online was both good and bad. Good because I could technically access it anytime, bad because there was no physical accountability. The video content helped since I'm visual, but the sheer amount of content was overwhelming. Shorter, more digestible chunks would have been easier to handle when I was already stressed.
Q20: I think that's probably someone like me—logging in, clicking around, trying to figure out what's going on, but never actually completing things. You might spend an hour in the LMS just being confused or anxious, not producing anything. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes it's just panic browsing.
Q21: That makes sense to me. I know people who barely touched the LMS but studied from textbooks or other sources and did fine. For me, my exam score was okay even though my LMS activity was low because I did learn some things offline. The LMS isn't the only place learning happens.
Q22: A course log can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but it can't show if you actually understood anything. It can't measure the anxiety that made you close the tab after two minutes. It misses offline studying, emotional state, life circumstances—basically everything that makes you human and not just a data point.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in effort because you want to understand, not just because you have to. For me, I wasn't really engaged this semester—I was surviving. There's a difference between going through the motions and actually being invested in your education.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I was dealing with high anxiety and sadness throughout the course. It wouldn't show the nights I couldn't sleep because I was worried about falling behind. It wouldn't show that I actually tried the mindfulness exercises and that they helped a little, even if my grades didn't reflect it. There's a whole person behind those login numbers.
